data.title: American Century Strategic Allocation Funds Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Strategic Allocation Portfolios all produced strong positive returns, while all three underperformed their benchmarks.

Market Environment

Global stocks and bonds rose during the third quarter. The Federal Reserve (Fed), reacting to job market weakness, reduced its benchmark interest rate for the first time this year. Otherwise, economic growth persisted, which helped stocks overcome concerns about inflation, a U.S. government shutdown and ongoing tariff uncertainties. U.S. stocks outperformed non-U.S. developed markets equities, and U.S. bonds outperformed investment-grade non-U.S. bonds.

U.S. and non-U.S. stocks advanced strongly. Small-cap stocks outperformed large- and mid-cap stocks. In the large-cap space, growth stocks outperformed value, but value stocks outperformed growth in the mid- and small-cap categories. Emerging markets stocks outperformed U.S. equities, benefiting from attractive relative valuations, interest rate cuts and a weaker dollar. Developed non-U.S. equities also enjoyed a strong quarter but lagged U.S. large-cap stocks.

Bond prices rose and contributed to portfolio diversification. The normalization of bond yields in recent years has helped restore the traditional role of fixed-income assets in diversified portfolios. Attractive yields and Fed rate cuts made for a good quarter for bond investors across many sectors.

Key Contributors

Every underlying allocation rose and contributed to absolute performance. In such a strong period for financial markets, performance varied widely by asset class, with U.S. large-cap stocks contributing most.

Select equity holdings buoyed relative performance. Manager selection added the most value in Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF (AVDV), Avantis International Equity ETF (AVDE) and Focused Large Cap Value (AVLV).

Modest contribution from bonds. Global Bond rose in absolute terms but fractionally underperformed its benchmark.

Key Detractors

Select growth holdings lagged. Manager selection was negative overall for U.S. and non-U.S. equities, with Small Cap Growth and U.S. Quality Growth ETF weighing on relative returns the most.

Positioning for the Future

Interest rate, tax and policy incentives support stocks. Fed rate cuts, new, lower tax rates and incentives to invest in the U.S. economy make a constructive backdrop for many stocks, though economic and trade uncertainties remain.

Diversification and risk management remain central to successful approaches. Uncertainty remains high, and with stock valuations elevated, we think it’s reasonable to expect more volatility before year-end, putting an emphasis on risk management.

Favoring U.S. equities over emerging markets. A resilient U.S. economy, monetary and fiscal stimulus and a positive corporate earnings backdrop explain our overweight to U.S. stocks relative to the benchmark.

In fixed income, focusing on valuation and quality. We prefer investment-grade over high-yield bonds because of relative valuations, narrow yield spreads and macro factors.

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPS): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest- paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher- yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities ((MBS)): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool. ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds (maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic (daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

data.title: Kinross: High-Margin Cash Machine With A Decade Of Growth Ahead
data.description:
Thesis

I found Kinross Gold (KGC) on Seeking Alpha in August 2025, and I have diligently been checking on the stock. It is one that has held well amid the recent pullback and has jumped around 50% since I first began looking at it. According to SA’s overview page, the stock is regarded as a “buy” by both Wall Street and SA analysts and a strong buy according to quant. I also think there is more room for the stock to grow in 2026; hence, I am also bullish.

The aforementioned growth, in my view, will not necessarily come from a big jump in production volumes—management actually guides relatively stable production of ~2.0 million gold-equivalent ounces annually for 2025–2027. Instead, I expect growth to be driven by structurally higher realized gold prices vs. 2023–24 levels, high incremental margins at core assets like Tasiast and the Paracatu, and increasing per-share metrics as the company retires shares through its buyback program.

Hence, Kinross Gold isn’t just a leveraged gold-trade since it is a high-margin, cash-rich senior producer with stable output and strong FCF with a generous capital return program (dividend payouts + share buybacks). Although the price of gold has been growing exponentially, especially in 2025 Kinross’s economics still hold up strong.

High-Quality Producer with Long Reserve Life and Strong Economics

Kinross Gold Corp. is a gold producer that explores for mines and processes gold and silver ore. The company mines in the US, Mauritania, Brazil, Chile and is expanding operations in Canada with the Great Bear project. - diluting geopolitical risks. Kinross also boasts high production volumes, with ~2.1 million gold equivalent ounces produced in 2024. At year-end 2024, company’s total proven and probable mineral reserve estimates decreased to 21.9 million ounces. At guided production levels for 2025-2027 at ~2 million ounces per year, the reserves are to hold up for at least the next decade (not factoring additional exploration success).

Although the reserves may seem on the lower end, as reserves of Newmont (NEM) are reported at 134 million ounces and Barrick Mining (B), which boasts 89 million ounces, are expected to last for more than a decade, other mid-tier gold miners such as AngloGold Ashanti (AU) reported 31.2 million ounces, and Pan American Silver (PAAS) has ~7 million ounces. So Kinross fares somewhere in the middle on that front.

More importantly, Tasiast in Mauritania and Paracatu in Brazil anchor long-life open-pit operations, while La Coipa in Chile and the U.S. portfolio (Fort Knox, Round Mountain, Bald Mountain, and Kinross’s 70% share of Manh Choh) provide additional diversification (operations overview). This spread of jurisdictional and asset risk is a key part of my bullish case: Kinross can sustain its ~2 million oz/year profile across multiple cycles without being forced into dilutive acquisitions just to maintain volume.

Massive Margins at Current Gold Prices

The recent gold price hike has transformed Kinross into a cash-flow powerhouse, as exemplified in the Q3 2025 earnings. There they report a realized avg. gold price of $3,460/oz and an AISC of $1,500/oz. In other words, ≈ $2,000/oz (58%) in margin. Extraordinarily high results for a cyclical mining company.

The kicker here is, according to my math, even if the gold prices retract from current levels and result in something like $2,400/oz, Kinross would still earn (assuming AISC stability) $900/oz or ~37% in margin. My AISC stability assumption derives from management commentary and expectations for cost stability, but more importantly, the consistent performance of the mines in Brazil and Mauritania. This is also the foundation of my resilient margin assumption, as Tasiast benefits from the completed 24k expansion and Paracatu's large-scale, low-strip operations, which continue to deliver constant cost performance - a portfolio cushion against cost inflation at higher-cost mines.

If gold prices drop, margins would be thinner. I, however, believe that costs would remain relatively stable (as this is management's expectation as well). Hence, even if gold does not remain at the current price levels, Kinross would still hold a margin percentage in perhaps the low-mid 30s. This is important since the company can remain solvent without necessitating any fundraising through stock dilutions.

Explosive FCF + Capital returns

KGC generated record FCF and the company now maintains a $1.7 billion in cash and cash equivalents section on the balance sheet. On the other hand, this high net-cash position allows management to continue their aggressive buyback programs. The current buyback authorization is already accounted for by TTM FCF, which gives the company room to keep retiring shares while de-risking the balance sheet. With the recent target for buybacks and dividends raised to $600 million and the record high FCF generation, I expect these programs to at least be maintained in 2026, given that production guidance is stable at 2.0Moz for 2025–2027, today’s elevated gold price environment continues, and the capex profile is largely manageable after the big Tasiast and Manh Choh build-outs and the upcoming Great Bear Project.

Balance Sheet Strengthening

Gold miners often self-destruct during boom times by leveraging up, however, we see the opposite trend with Kinross, as the balance sheet reflects that the company holds more cash than debt and liquidity, standing over $3 billion. Hence, Kinross now has the ability to withstand gold price corrections without necessitating dilutions, but by reducing buyback programs and capex. This flexibility to withstand crisis without compromising the balance sheet is a very strong indicator for strong long-term resilience, especially for a cyclical commodity producer.

Operational Stability and Growth

The crown jewel in Kinross’ portfolio is undoubtedly their mine in Mauritania - the Tasiast. It is one of the highest-margin large-scale mines globally. Recently the company disclosed record throughput and production levels (~622k oz for 2024) and lower unit costs due to scale efficiencies - cost of sales is $681/oz vs other sites ranging from ~$1000/oz to $1500/oz. Kinross expects the mine’s lifecycle to extend to 2033.

The Paracatu is a mine in Brazil and stands as one of the largest in the country. However, the low cost of sales in the Tasiast mine, has managed to produce 500k oz+ yearly for 7 consecutive years, making it a pillar of stability in the Kinross portfolio.

These two mines manage to provide stability and margin upside in fluctuating environments. However, their other sites are also boasting high asset life expectancies and have provided reliable track records with consistent performance, as La Coipa is giving some promising results - management expects lower unit costs in late 2025 and production of Phase 7 ore in the near future.

However, currently the Great Bear Project is their most exciting and promising long term opportunity. Preliminary results indicate a cost of sales of $594/oz (beating Tasiast) and an AISC of $800 for the first 8 years. The project also boasts continuing high-grade drilling results, clear path towards an initial mine, significant potential to become a multi-decade tier one asset. This is a free call option on future production growth.

These developments present 2 driving forces to help maintain low costs and expand revenues in 2026 and 2027. First are the stability drivers from Paracatu, core US-based mines, and La Coipa (once Phase 7 ore is fully on stream). As these mines would more than likely maintain steady, predictable production values combined with stable operational costs. Then there are growth drivers, the Tasiast through continued optimization, and the biggest upside catalyst in my opinion is when Great Bear begins production, which is scheduled for 2029.

Hence, volume growth will most likely remain as is for the next 2 years, but production output will increase when currently undertaken projects begin operations. That being said, it is important to also note that the gold market is favorable, and the (probable) continued increase in the commodity’s price would more than likely support Kinross’s revenue growth by expanding margins.

Valuation

KGC is not trading at any euphoric multiples (KGC valuation). Non-GAAP (FWD) P/E is 15.37 vs sector median of 15.93 and GAAP PEG is 0.13. (FWD) P/S is elevated at 4.48 vs 1.34 median. Although this is a mixed picture, the FCF yield suggests that the stock is trading at a discount. The TTM FCF is $1.81 billion, and the market cap as of the time of writing is $31.38 billion. Dividing the TTM FCF by the market cap gives us the TTM FCF yield, which in this case is 5%. For reference, the average SPY 500 FCF yield is around 3-4%. Assuming that trends continue (gold price rising and FCF growing), this yield is likely to remain stable or even increase in the future; hence, the company can continue providing stable capital returns for years to come.

Fair Value

Using the FWD EV/EBITDA framework, Kinross still trades at a noticeable discount to senior gold peers. KGC currently trades at roughly 7.5× FWD EBITDA, whereas the industry average is at ~9x. Using this industry multiple, the implied upside is significant: moving from 7.5× to 9.5× represents a valuation increase of ~20% (9/7.5=1.20) - supporting a fair value in the $32–$33 per share range before incorporating additional catalysts like the Great Bear Project.

Risks

Although all looks promising for Kinross, there are some risks that are to be acknowledged that could undermine my thesis. If gold dropped back to less than $1,800/oz and stayed there, margins would compress and the FCF narrative wouldn’t hold anymore. Cost inflation (labor, energy, royalty) is another potential risk that could diminish margins. Setbacks in Great Bear or Tasiast would hurt investor confidence and put pressure on the stock.

Final Take

Kinross is not just riding a gold mania wave, since it is converting record gold prices into tangible shareholder value through their dividend and buyback programs. The company also boasts high margins, net-cash balance sheet, strong and diversified operations combined with massive FCF.

In short, the core of my bullish thesis is that Kinross gives you: (1) stable, multi-asset production of around 2.0Moz per year, (2) very strong margins at today’s gold prices with room to remain profitable in a lower gold price scenario, (3) a rapidly improving balance sheet and rising capital returns, and (4) meaningful long-term growth optionality through Great Bear—all at a valuation that still prices the company more like a normal cyclical miner than a high-margin, net-cash compounder.

At current valuations, KGC offers investors exposure to gold upside without having to pay a premium. Even if gold prices retract, KGC is still protected and would hold some high margins and remain profitable. I would recommend this stock for investors who like gold, but are not willing to chase high bubble-like prices or don’t want to chase speculative mining companies or ETFs. For me, Kinross remains a compelling buy.

data.title: Rheinmetall: Here's Why I'm Upgrading Europe's Defense Powerhouse Stock To Strong Buy
data.description:
Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY) (RHM:CA) is one of the European defense companies that has shown remarkable performance since I initiated coverage in August 2023. The stock price has surged over 530%, easily outperforming the S&P 500. The main driver, of course, is the demand for defense equipment in Europe. While the stock price performance has been impressive, we note that the stock price has come down by 11.4% since my last report, driven by peace plans in Europe. Rheinmetall AG recently hosted its investor day, detailing its growth plans towards 2030. In this report, I review the plans and update my price target for Rheinmetall stock.

Defense Spending And Expansion Of Military Domains Support Growth

The obvious growth driver for Rheinmetall is the increase in defense budgets globally. The NATO target is a spending of 5% of GDP by 2035, and Germany is targeting 3.5% by 2029. Those defense budgets are expanding due to the elevated threat levels. While there currently are prospects of a peace plan for Ukraine, the reality is that on the European mainland we are seeing more and more disruptions affecting military and transport infrastructure. Examples are drone sightings in Poland, Belgium, the Netherlands, Denmark, Germany, and Romania. Besides that, even if there is a sustainable peace plan for Ukraine, Europe has to catch up on their investments in defense.

For Rheinmetall, we note that the company remains focused on the land domain. However, it will also be expanding its activity and product portfolio in the air, space, and naval domains. One example is the acquisition of Naval Vessels Lürssen for an undisclosed price expected to be finalized in 2026.

Transformational Growth For Rheinmetall

Rheinmetall will be organizing its business into new segments starting in 2026. These units are:

  • Vehicle Systems
  • Weapon and Ammunition
  • Air Defense
  • Digital
  • Naval after the acquisition closes

The focus on new domains such as sea and digital adds to the company’s margin level. In 2023, Rheinmetall had margins of 12.8%. The biggest segment was Vehicle Systems, which accounted for 36.3% of the sales and margins of 12.4%. The company expects to have €13 billion to €15 billion in sales for that segment by 2030, with margins of 14% at the midpoint. So, there is a bit of margin lift compared to 2023 driven by higher volumes, but it is not huge. The other segment that already was part of Rheinmetall in 2023 is Weapons and Ammunitions. That business generated €1.76 billion in sales with margins of 23%. By 2030, the company expects €14 billion to €16 billion in sales, making it bigger than the Vehicle Systems segment, and margins will expand to 29%-31%. Air Defense is expected to generate €3 billion to €4 billion in sales with 25% margins at the midpoint, while Digital will generate sales of €8 billion to €9 billion with 18% margins at the midpoint, and Naval will be around €5 billion in sales with margins of more than 15%. The expected consolidated margins will be 20%, driven by volume and diversification into other domains.

In the Vehicle Systems segment, sales are expected to be driven by higher Boxer and tracked infantry fighting vehicles and, to a lesser extent, medium wheeled and main battle tank sales. In Weapons and Ammunitions, artillery sales will be the major growth driver, followed by rockets and medium-caliber ammunition.

Air Defense sales are driven by short-range air defense systems, which support protection against drones. Especially in recent months, we have seen drones causing problems around key infrastructure in Europe. With the Skynet and Skyranger drone interceptors and the Skyspotter, Rheinmetall is positioned to capitalize on counter-UAS demand.

A digital approach towards the battlefield is going to support the Digital segment's growth with modern communication solutions as a key growth driver. The company is also expanding its aerospace services.

So, we see broad-based growth, and Rheinmetall has already strengthened its business through acquisitions and joint ventures. That positions the company to become a €50 billion revenue company with margins of more than 50%. So, we see plenty of opportunities. The primary risks are subdued investor sentiment due to peace talks, as we see now, and potentially reduced demand for defense equipment. However, with the significant underinvestment over the past decades, I do believe that reducing defense budgets as soon as peace in Ukraine is achieved is fragile, especially since any peace that may flow out of current talks is likely to be extremely fragile.

Rheinmetall Stock Is A Strong Buy For The Near And Long Term

Based on the expectations that the company presented during the investor day, I have modeled growth expectations, and if Rheinmetall executes successfully on that growth path, we see an investment opportunity that I would label as “once in a lifetime.” The company is expected to continue increasing its volumes, driving operating leverage that leads to substantial growth in EBITDA and free cash flow. In the years ahead, capital expenditures are expected to remain elevated, and that is without accounting for cash inflows from joint-venture-funded capital expenditures. By 2029, capital expenditure levels should normalize as a percentage of operating cash flow. I have modeled in a rather modest dividend increase trajectory and have not assumed any share repurchases. However, if there is no major M&A besides the acquisition in Naval partially offset by divesting the Power Systems business, there is substantial space to repurchase shares, as the company is expected to enter a net cash position by 2027. So, the company is already seeing strong growth in its financial results, but that is expected to be “just the start” of it.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Stock price targets are based on the ordinary shares.

If we look at the near-term upside, we see that the stock is fairly valued with 2026 earnings in mind, and I do believe that makes sense. Many stocks do not trade with current earnings in mind but the earnings one year ahead. As we are close to rolling into 2026, looking at upside driven by the expected earnings for 2027 makes sense, and then we see that there is a 49% upside, which I believe makes for a compelling strong buy case. The stock is substantially overvalued against the company’s median EV/EBITDA; however, the business environment has changed in such a way that I do not believe that the company's median EV/EBITDA multiple is applicable. Looking beyond 2027, the uncertainty on the growth does increase as execution risk simply increases. However, if Rheinmetall executes as targeted, we see an impressive 290% growth opportunity.

Conclusion: Reason To Be Bullish On Rheinmetall

Rheinmetall is already considered the powerhouse of the European defense industry, and driven by acquisitions, operating leverage, and joint ventures, its position is expected to become even more dominant, driving further upside for Rheinmetall stock. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy. One potential risk for Rheinmetall is a reduction in share prices driven by sentiment due to possible peace in Ukraine. However, that does not discount the fact that European countries are decades behind on making substantial investments in their defense weapon systems.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ACWI: Core Global Equities Strategy
data.description:
The iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI) is a passively managed strategy designed to track large- and mid-cap companies in developed and emerging markets. The strategy can largely be utilized by passive investors seeking broad exposure to US and international equities, though it maintains similar characteristics to US indexes, exposing investors to the technology sector and mega-cap technology stocks.

About iShares MSCI ACWI ETF

ACWI was launched by iShares on March 26, 2008, on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 32 bps, aligned with peer global ETFs. ACWI pays out a modest distribution rate of $2.03/share, yielding 1.45% for the last twelve-month period. ACWI is broadly held with $24b in net assets and an average trading volume of $434mm in share value.

ACWI is benchmarked to the MSCI ACWI Index, which covers mid- and large-cap equities in developed and emerging markets. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index, meaning that US large-cap equities may be prioritized given the relative size of these constituents.

Accordingly, the Index is most heavily weighted towards the technology sector, accounting for 28.5% of the total Index.

ACWI is broadly diversified across 2,280 holdings, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 25% of net assets. Narrowing this down, the Magnificent 7 stocks account for 22.51% of net assets, suggesting significant concentration to the top holdings within the strategy. Though this doesn’t amount to the same level of concentration exhibited in the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which sits at 48.26% for the Mag 7 stocks, I believe the concentration is still large with respect to the number of holdings within the portfolio. The large concentration in the top 10 holdings may result in these stocks having more influence over the direction of the total portfolio with respect to the remaining 75% of the holdings.

Thematically, I believe the technology sector has room for continued growth, particularly as the AI and cloud hosting theme remains intact following the Q3’26 earnings results by Nvidia (NVDA). On a global scale, I have reason to believe that the AI theme may be expanding across geographies beyond the US and China, driven by sovereign nations investing in localized infrastructure. This will likely drive further investment into companies tied to the broader market theme, including building infrastructure for constructing data centers, IT infrastructure for outfitting data centers, and power transmission and distribution for supplying power to data centers, reflecting the growth expected in the US over the coming years. Though investments will likely benefit US AI infrastructure companies like Nvidia most, given the technology advancements over the rest of the market, investing in developing localized data centers may provide benefits to local economies for the construction, energy, and industrials markets.

Investor Suitability

Net of the top technology holdings, ACWI is a well-diversified strategy that covers a broad range of holdings across each sector, providing minimal exposure to any single stock. ACWI can be utilized as a core investment, acting as the bedrock for building upon. I believe the best way to utilize ACWI is to build satellite positions on top of the core ACWI holding in order to gain exposure to underrepresented sectors that may appeal to the investor, such as energy or utilities.

ACWI can also be held as a placeholder as an equity position as investors seek out new investment opportunities, gaining beta growth in the meantime. For example, if an investor wishes to invest more heavily in the energy sector using an industry indexed strategy like the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), the investor could proportionally reduce exposure to ACWI while allocating to XLE.

At a high level, I do not believe ACWI will be an alpha-generating strategy given the broad diversification across constituents.

Fund Comparison

There are a number of passively managed core strategies investors may choose from to gain broad equity exposure, including the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (VT), Avantis All Equity Markets ETF (AVGE), or the Capital Group Global Equity ETF (CGGE), among others.

Though launched in 2024, CGGE has outperformed peer equity strategies on a 1-year, total return basis.

Comparing 10-year performance, which will not include CGGE or the Pacer MSCI World Industry Advantage ETF (GLBL), VT offers similar performance at a significantly lower fee of 6bps. Given the similar performance and exposure to global equities, VT may be a more appealing option given the low fee.

Risks Related to ACWI

ACWI is a broadly diversified, passively managed equity strategy that will provide investors with global exposure. Given the broad diversification, ACWI will mostly exhibit beta risk, or risk in association with the broader market. Investors must consider the significant concentration in US equities, the technology sector, and the mega-cap technology stocks held within the strategy, particularly when building a diversified portfolio around the ETF. Given the substantial concentration in these categories, US index exposure may exhibit substantial concentration risk, particularly in the mega-cap technology stocks.

Final Thoughts

ACWI can be an appealing core equity holding, providing investors with broad, global diversification across countries and industries. From an allocation perspective, ACWI can be utilized for gaining US and international equity exposure as a core holding. ACWI may also be used as an equity placeholder in between trades, providing beta growth as investors search for new investment ideas. Given the relative performance and fees, I am recommending investors consider VT as an alternative; however, given beta growth, I am recommending ACWI with a Buy rating.

data.title: Frontline: Rising Spot Rates Favoring Company's Cash Guidance Into 2026
data.description:
Shares of shipping company, Frontline plc (FRO), are up 32.62% (YoY) and 82.52% (YTD). The increase is despite Frontline’s Q3 2025 revenues declining 9.87% (QoQ), while net profits were down 48% (QoQ). I believe the increase is partly due to rising demand for tankers globally and growing shipments of crude oil, with buyers said to be exploring alternatives to Russian oil. With this in mind, I will explain why I’m rating this stock as a hold, coupled with the fact that it is trading at fair value.

Firstly, let’s explore the idea of growing tanker rates into 2026. Daily tanker rates have hit a 5-year high with supertankers said to be charging a historic rate of $137,000. In Q3 2025, Frontline attained a mean daily spot time charter equivalent (TCE) rate of $34,500 for VLCCs, $35,100 for Suezmax tankers and $31,400 for LR2/Aframax tankers. On sequential analysis, there was a 7.17% (QoQ) increase in the average TCE rates offered on chartering LR2/Aframax as compared to Frontline’s VLCCs and Suezmax tankers.

As noted earlier, on the Bloomberg report, there has been high tanker rates in the Middle East due to heightened crude oil production from OPEC+ producer states. The increased demand for crude oil from the Middle East (as an alternative to Russian oil) is set to continue forcing tanker rates to historic levels into 2026. By the end of October 2025, the daily average spot rates for VLCCs operating in the Middle East Gulf-China trade routes soared 42% (YoY) to $125,081.

This graph shows that into Q4 2025, the global daily average TCE levels (which at the time the graph was drawn stood at $108,833) were at levels last seen in April 2020. Reports further indicate that the MEG-East Coast India trade route registered a historic daily average TCE of $167,357.

Further, the larger vessel freight rates are rising across the board, affecting both oil/product tankers and dry bulk carriers. There has been a positive movement in the Baltic Dry Index (BDI) that shows the movement of raw materials (apart from oil) by sea.

The BDI stands at 2,295 points and is up 50.10% (YoY), showing the rising daily cost of shipping mineral items such as coal, iron ore and many more, which inherently means positing sea activity into 2026. For instance, dry bulk carrier Genco Shipping & Trading Limited is up 37.66% (YTD) and has risen 160% in the last 5 years. Another carrier, Star Bulk Carriers, has increased 32.11% (YTD) showing positive market sentiment into 2026. Further, FRO is dependent on the Baltic exchange rates in establishing its cash projections, as explained in its Q3 2025 earnings call, when it gave a cash guidance of $1.8 billion based on the Baltic Exchange recorded on November 18, 2025.

Order Book growth

Frontline expects to grow its order book into 2026 and 2027. As at Q3 2025, FRO’s tanker asset order book consisted of, and I quote “20.3% of the existing global fleet, with 135, 111, and 162 vessels on order for VLCCs, Suezmax tankers and LR2 tankers, respectively.” This is a significant number considering there are about 3 deliveries expected to be made in Q4 2025. As a replacement strategy, FRO is looking to maintain a consistent fleet growth at a CAGR of 15% (between 2025 to 2029). Then again, we have the introduction of eco-vessels, or a fleet of about 80 vessels that are energy efficient, with 56% of the fleet scrubber-fitted. We are looking at lower operational costs in 2026 since expenses took up more than 90% of FRO’s revenue, lowering earnings by $392.4 million. Additionally, FRO’s eco-vessels have an average age of 7.2 years, which is a young fleet considering Okeanis Eco Tankers, founded in 2018, has an environmentally compliant fleet with an average age of 4.6 years. At the onset, Frontline has a regulatory advantage as far as environmental compliance is concerned and is expecting higher demand from buyers and higher resale chances.

Overall, the crude oil tankers market is expected to reach $250 billion by 2032, from $180 billion in 2023, growing at a CAGR of 3.5% (between 2023-2025).

Valuation

I will use the price-to-earnings ratio to evaluate Frontline as it will incorporate the company’s net income against the share price. It will also help identify if the stock is potentially undervalued or overvalued, thereby determining how much the market can pay for the earnings into 2026.

FRO’s forward PE metric stands at 13.89X against the earnings per share (EPS) of 1.81. The computation of these two gives us the new share price of $25.14. As of this writing, FRO’s share price stood at $24.96 (a difference of +0.72%), meaning that the stock is trading at a fair valuation.

I will use the metric to compare the performance of FRO with some of its peers. We have to keep in mind that the PE sector median sits at 13.63X.

Sector Median (13.63X) Forward PE ratio Forward EPS Future Share price Current Share Price Valuation Dividend yield
FRO 13.89X $1.81 $25.14 $24.96 +0.72% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 3.04%
CMB.TECH NV. (CMBT) 46.14X $0.22 $10.15 $10.05 +1.0% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 0.49%
DHT Holdings, Inc. (DHT) 13.43X $0.99 $13.30 $13.06 +1.84% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 5.41%
Teekay Tankers Ltd. (TNK) 9.45X $6.45 $60.95 $59.48 +2.5% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 3.28%

The tabulated analysis shown above indicates that FRO and its peers are trading at their fair valuation, that is as far as their PE ratios are concerned. However, DHT is offering the highest annual dividend yield at 5.41% while CMBT has the lowest yield at 0.49%.

However, back to Frontline, the company stated that it had a cash generation potential of 30,000 on an annual basis, thereby giving guidance of $1.8 billion for the FY 2025. An increase at a range of 30% to 44.4% from the current average spot rate would raise the cash generated by the end of 2025 to $2.6 billion. It represents a cash growth of 519% (YoY) from $419.6 million realised in the FY 2024. FRO’s free cash flow as of December 2024 stood at $736.4 million. Therefore, an increase of 30% to 44.4% in FY 2025 will raise its FCF to the range of $957.32 million to $1.06 billion. Keeping in mind that FRO does not have any significant debts maturing before 2030, thereby allowing the company to retain more cash.

I would also like to consider FRO's cash breakeven rates into Q4 2025 and for the full year 2025.

On quarterly analysis, Frontline has surpassed its daily average cash breakeven rates into 2026 with the exception of it's Q1 2025 LR2/Aframax average daily spot TCE that fell 5.83% (YoY). While the numbers were stronger in 2024 (see table for comparison), all the average daily rates that is, $38,200 (VLCC), $35,100 (Suezmax) and $27,900 (LR2/Aframax) all exceeded the daily average cash breakeven rates for the 12 months of 2025. The main concern here is that, into Q4 2025; FRO had only covered 51% of its average daily TCE for LR2/Aframax which is equal to $21,522 indicating that it almost 10% short of the daily average cash breakeven rate of $23,600.

Risk

This valuation is dependent on the growth of tanker rates and positive Baltic Exchange rates that will ensure FRO realises higher earnings into 2026. A decline in the exchange rates and the spot TCEs will have an adverse effect on FRO’s valuation, as elucidated in this article.

Bottom Line

I have rated Frontline as a hold based on the rising shipments of crude oil away from Russia and growing tanker demand that has forced an increase in spot TCE rates. However, the stock is trading at a fair valuation but has the capacity to grow its free cash flow levels to $1.06 billion from a potential cash generation of $2.6 billion based on the company’s forecast of a 30% increase to the current spot rates into 2026.

data.title: Rates Spark: A Good Place For Rates And Sentiment
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Steady EUR rates and market sentiment with ECB in a good place, but risks of more easing likely understated

EUR rates are staying true to their ranges, even if testing out the upper regions of late, with the 10y swap rate just above 2.7%. Away from the ultra-long end, which had resumed its steepening at the start of this month, the curve also remains more range-bound. In fact, 2s10s is bang on the post-'Liberation Day' average.

Risk perception of broader markets has also returned to a more benign setting, with equities having recouped some of their recent losses. In rates markets, the implied volatilities have edged lower again, which in turn has helped bond spreads to re-tighten. Over the past few days, 10y Italian government bonds have marked their lowest spreads over Bunds since 2010. Even French bonds are seeing their tightest spreads since late August, when political turmoil began to first hit the market.

Markets could be interpreted as a reflection of the ECB's wait-and-see mode. The ECB minutes of the October meeting just reaffirmed that the central bank sees itself in a “good place.” On closer inspection, the ECB still holds a slight easing bias, and expectations of a gradual recovery are still surrounded by risks and uncertainties, such as around trade or the implementation of government stimulus and investment plans. Our economists also highlight the potential downward impact on inflation projections of the EU’s plans to delay the next phase of the emissions trading system.

On the back of this, we also think that short-end rates should move back to incorporate higher chances of more ECB easing in their pricing. For now, the market is looking at around a one-in-three chance of one more ECB cut over the course of next year. For the broader curve, that means a bias to re-steepen in our view. That also remains our longer-run view if the picture of a gradual recovery is confirmed and longer rates gradually move higher to test above the top of current ranges.

Friday’s events and market view

The US is still off, which should again make for quieter markets. Out of the eurozone, we will get the first country indications for the November CPI, with France, Spain, Italy and Germany reporting their preliminary figures. Overall, eurozone inflation is expected to stay little changed, with the consensus looking for a 2.1% YoY reading next week. Separately, the ECB will also release the results of its consumer survey on longer-run inflation expectations today, where the 1y horizon figure is anticipated to ease lower towards 2.6%.

No issuance is scheduled for today. In terms of scheduled rating reviews, S&P still has France on its calendar, but since it downgraded the rating to A+/Stable just last month, it is likely this data will be skipped.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Newegg Commerce: Fundamentals Do Not Justify The Valuation, Sell
data.description:
Introduction

Newegg Commerce (NEGG) has seen an amazing turnaround in its share price this past year, something that cannot be said about its financial situation. It seems the company is yet another meme stock that sees a lot of volatility, and I would recommend selling right now if you still own it, as the business model in the long term doesn’t seem viable without proper cost-cutting initiatives.

The Company

NEGG is an e-commerce company that sells PC hardware, consumer and enterprise electronics. Core offerings include desktops, laptops, PC components like GPUs and CPUs, motherboards, gaming peripherals, etc. It is a B2C and B2B platform.

The company makes its money through direct sales, so it buys up inventory and then resells it. It’s a rather straightforward business model.

Briefly on Financial Performance

Looking at NEGG’s sales performance over the last two years or so, we can see a downtrend that has been somewhat broken at the end of 2024, which is a good sign, but doesn’t necessarily tell us that everything is coming back up now. It is a good start. So far, the company has only reported its first half of the year performance, showing us that there has been a decent increase in sales of almost 13%, which was primarily driven by increased demand for GPUs, like Nvidia’s (NVDA) GeForce RTX 50 Series, and AMD’s (AMD) Radeon RX 9000 series GPUs and CPUs.

During the same time, GMV increased by around 13.7% to $849m, most likely driven by the same products as mentioned above. Average volume increased to $467 vs $401 in the same period last year, with repeat purchase rate increasing to 25.2% compared to 23% last year. Repeat purchaser is an active customer who has made at least two purchases on the website in the last six months.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, the company operates in a very tight margin business sector, and even with such an improvement in sales, the company is still not turning a profit; however, we can see that the first half of 2025 improved quite a bit due to the refreshed GPU and CPU sales. But even then, it doesn’t look like the company managed to turn a profit, which is concerning. Even the largest releases of the new GPUs and CPUs weren’t enough to help it turn green. EBITDA, which is a non-GAAP metric, is also negative.

If we adjusted the numbers further, which I am not a fan of, but most companies tend to do it to show a better picture, Adjusted EBITDA improved quite a bit from last year and turned positive, which is the only positive thing regarding its operations so far. We can see that stock-based compensation is one of the bigger items that affect the company.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation on its balance sheet. NEGG finished the quarter with around $59m in cash and equivalents against $15m in short-term debt, and $47m in lease liabilities. It’s worth noting that the company’s cash position decreased to $59m from $96m, and that is mainly due to the company’s inventory buildup. Compared to last year, the company’s inventory increased by a substantial amount, going from around $98.5m to $152m. Is it worrying? It is hard to tell right now, because the management is saying that it is “taking advantage of accelerated spending amid tariff uncertainties” and is working with partners to limit the tariff impact. So, in a way, they have an excuse to have the elevated inventory levels right now, fully expecting to sell through it, but it has to be monitored further.

Overall, the company’s recent financial performance doesn’t strike me as really good and definitely doesn’t justify such a share price appreciation. Additionally, the limited information for shareholders, with the lack of conference calls, is a bit disappointing. It is hard to get a decent picture of the company’s financials when it doesn’t provide a lot of information more often.

Comments on the Outlook

Another Meme Stock

As of right now, on Thanksgiving in the US, markets are closed, and so far this year, the company’s share price is up over 535%, sitting at around $76.5 per share. At one point this year, back in May, the company’s share price was as low as $3.32, which then skyrocketed to as high as $137.84. That’s a 4000% move from the lows in just two and a half months. Why the move? I remember browsing many financial subreddits and one of the bigger ones active right now, since the GameStop’s (GME) meme craze remains WallStreetBets. I remember seeing Newegg pop up quite a bit during the summer. Many financial media outlets during the same period labeled the company a new meme stock, noting that its run was driven primarily by social-media hype around the Gamer Community launch, the GPU buzz with NVDA. Being a rather lightly traded company, and at one point saw its short interest spike to over 300%, it was ripe for a meme stock short squeeze event.

In reality, all this news did very little to improve the company’s financial outlook. The company announced its full-year financial outlook, and the management expects sales to be in the range of $1.375B-$1.424B and still report a loss on the bottom line.

Since topping almost $138 a share on literally nothing but social-media hype, the company’s stock price began to fall. It fell to a low of $30 in just a couple of weeks, and over the last 2 months recovered quite a bit, keeping the meme craze alive.

The AI Hype story is helping

Sure, there were some real catalysts at the beginning of the year, like the refresh cycle of GPUs and CPUs, but that wasn’t enough to push its shares so high. NVDA is the AI darling in this growth story, and since NEGG is selling a lot of NVDA’s products, there will always be some sort of mentality to follow NVDA’s money. The new RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell GPUs that the management mentioned are going to help the second half of the year in terms of sales, are not going to drive efficiency higher, and since NVDA doesn’t release upgrades too often, typically, once every couple of years, which means the company is not going to get that boost in sales in the first half of 2026 at least. This will make its financial performance come in lower than what we saw this past half of the year.

The good thing about this AI growth story is that NVDA continues to see an amazing demand for its products regarding data centers and AI, with over 62% growth y/y primarily driven by Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra platforms, and with all the big tech still spending a ridiculous amount of cash on the AI race, companies like Newegg will most likely see some a lot of share price volatility going forward, especially if they continue to market NVDA products front and center, but it doesn’t seem like the business is going to get a lot of boost in the performance. It just looks like this retail business is not viable even during the good times, as it just cannot turn a profit on the biggest product launches of the year.

I want to circle back to the company’s inventory levels, which I think are more than just gearing up for a heavy season of buying. Sure, I can see some more sales coming through because the second half of the year is going to be full of holidays like Black Friday, Christmas, and some other holidays I’m sure I missed. Is the company going to sell through all of this, or is it a sign of things to come? I am going to eagerly await the next report to find out.

The most recent news regarding new developments with the company is that they announced a partnership with PayPal (PYPL) to offer an AI-powered shopping experience through portals like Perplexity. So, essentially agent AI service will allow customers to purchase PC components through an AI search engine. The news wasn’t big enough to make the company’s share surge, and I think this is not going to be such a big deal in the end, too.

Closing Comments

To sum up my feelings about the company, it doesn’t strike me as a fundamentally strong company with rational behavior regarding its share price. This is one of those companies that real long-term investors should avoid if they would like to preserve their wealth and not chase at the top. Sometimes it may be fun to plop down some cash that you got lying around but expect to lose most of it if you are chasing the momentum.

I expect to see further volatility because of AI and NVDA being a thing still, and because of the company’s reminiscence of gamer vibes comparable to GameStop, which will continue to bring upswings through the next meme craze, which seems to happen every month or so now.

The company’s business model doesn’t seem to support it at all, even when sales are through the roof. The company needs to find ways to cut costs and make it a lot more attractive fundamentally. It’s just not a viable business otherwise. I’m avoiding it, and I would recommend selling it while it is still very overvalued unless you don’t like money.

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

  • Global equities rose strongly in U.S. dollar terms on trade war de-escalation and signs of resilient economic growth in many countries.
  • U.S. market performance was especially strong, aided by expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts. The Fed reduced policy rates in September, with additional rate cuts expected by year-end. Stocks in Japan and the emerging markets also performed well, although European equities lagged after outperforming earlier in the year.
  • The market advance was relatively narrow, with notable gains in information technology stocks that rose on excitement around artificial intelligence (AI). Real estate was the weakest-performing market sector.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by a lack of exposure to several stocks that were very strong performers for the index. These included companies viewed as beneficiaries of AI. As investors have become more excited about the potential around the AI complex, they have driven shares of even indirect beneficiaries of AI-related spending to higher and higher valuations that we believe are unsupported by company fundamentals. While not owning these stocks detracted from relative performance, we remain steadfast in seeking to protect our investors from excessive risk, including valuation risk.

U.S.-based fast casual restaurant chain Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) was a notable detractor from relative performance. We originally invested in Chipotle because of its track record of operational efficiencies and digital leadership. In recent quarters, however, the company has faced business headwinds as cash-strapped consumers have cut back on spending at restaurants. As a result, the company reported two consecutive quarters of disappointing same-store sales growth, and it missed its earnings targets and reduced guidance. We have been disappointed with Chipotle’s recent execution, and we exited the position.

Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG) was another relative detractor, as insurance stocks have generally lagged in a market focused on higher growth investments. A global provider of insurance brokerage and risk management, Gallagher has taken advantage of industry fragmentation to grow its market share and strengthen its competitive position. The company has continued to deliver stable earnings growth and attractive returns on capital. We are also constructive on its accretive purchase of insurance broker AssuredPartners, which expands Gallagher's reach in the middle-market segment.

Contemporary Amperex Technology (OTC:CTATF) (CATL) was a top positive contributor to relative performance. This Chinese company controls more than 30% of the electric vehicle battery market, serving major manufacturers such as Tesla and Volkswagen. Additionally, it has emerged as a leader in energy storage solutions (ESS) that improve the efficiency and reliability of renewable energy for the power grid. CATL has signed several large ESS deals, including in China where authorities are targeting a significant increase in energy storage capacity by 2027. CATL has continued to generate significant free cash flow, which it is reinvesting in technology and returning to shareholders through stock buybacks.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM)(TSMC) was another notable contributor. As one of the world's dominant logic chip foundries, TSMC is a leading supplier of graphic processing units for companies, such as NVIDIA, that are at the forefront of the AI buildout. Excitement around the AI supply chain grew as leading hyperscalers and other technology companies continued to announce substantive investments in high-performance computing. We believe TSMC is well positioned to capitalize on this spending due to its unparalleled scale, deep technological advantages, and partnerships with major fabless semiconductor companies and hyperscalers. The company has further expanded its market leadership as competitors have stumbled or shifted away from the foundry business, leaving TSMC as the unparalleled market leader in this space. And yet, TSMC continues to trade at an attractive valuation discount to its customers.

Manager outlook

We have been reassured by the continued growth in the global economy, and we have been pleased to see many of our companies make progress on their growth trajectories. While we acknowledge economic headwinds, we believe interest rate cuts in the U.S. and other markets could help spur increased global economic activity. We continue to find compelling investment opportunities across sectors and geographies, and we remain excited about innovations we have seen in areas such as information technology, healthcare, and energy production and storage. At the same time, we caution that valuations in some pockets of the market appear increasingly disconnected from underlying fundamentals.

In this environment, we believe active management is critical to managing risk. We remain committed to sifting through opportunities to identify companies positioned for superior free-cash-flow growth that we believe is undervalued by the market. Our focus remains on companies with exceptional management teams, high-quality balance sheets, strong competitive positioning, and high or improving returns on invested capital. We have taken steps to cushion the portfolio against potential market volatility by investing in companies with recurring revenues and earnings visibility that we believe can outperform across market environments. We view this disciplined, fundamentals-driven approach as key to delivering long-term capital appreciation for our investors.
