data.title: Aeluma: Revenue Growth Meets Heavy Spending, Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction And Investment Thesis

I am downgrading Aeluma, Inc. (ALMU) to a Hold from my previous Buy rating, driven by the operational spending, such as manufacturing readiness and commercialization execution. I am still optimistic about Aeluma on a long-term view, looking at the latest (Q1 2026) spike in top-line growth, which rose from $481,000 to $1.4 million, representing a 191% increase YoY. Honestly, even though the stock is trending downwards, it has a promising future if you look at its spending. I find it aligned with the growth of the semiconductor industry, which I highlighted in my previous article. For example, as new contracts keep coming in, this suggests that the company is heading in the right direction.

However, in what I can consider to be a blessing in disguise, Aeluma has to pay a price to find solid ground for a sustained bullish reversal. The company is still investing more in operations like manufacturing equipment to execute the contracts I highlighted in my previous article. But for an investor, I am cautious because right now volume orders are yet to be secured, and once secured, the company may take 6 months to 12 months to stand up its outside capacity. This means that the current increased operational spending may not see a recoup of the value not later than 6 months to 12 months, which is when I expect a solid bullish reversal.

So far, the company’s stock price has plummeted by 19%, underperforming the S&P 500 since my previous article. With that said, I will delve deeper into the root of this to unravel why I think, as it stands now, this company warrants a hold.

Background Of High Operations Spending

Before I get to operations, I want to give you the context of high expenditure, which I think is the commitment to actions towards delivering the contract obligations. In my previous article, I highlighted that this company’s optimism is driven by how contracts keep coming in. I still believe in this perspective, as you can see, the contracts are still coming in, with the latest contract from NASA. In this contract, Aeluma is expected to deliver advanced Quantum Dot Photonics Integrated Circuits for AI applications and aerospace to NASA.

In this article, I have introduced that Aeluma estimates to stand up its outside capacity in around 6 to 12 months, which is within the expected time when it ramps up production to target markets from volume orders. When I dig deeper, I see that Aeluma joining the Midwest Microelectronics Consortium (MMEC) to speed up its pathway to commercial and defense markets is within this outlook. From these operations above, it leads me to understand why operating expenses skyrocketed in Q1 2026 to $2.29 million, representing a 33.92%.

Still, connecting the dots, when I look at the 6-12 months’ timeline when Aeluma is expected to stand up its outside capacity, it relates to the FY2026 guidance where the company seeks to expand its team by hiring more staff and leadership team. The company is looking forward to increasing its validation and test capabilities. There are no exact expenditure estimates, but from the rising expenses and the projected weaker EPS in the coming years, such as a -1,750% EPS growth in 2026, I believe the company is likely to keep investing.

While the immediate effect of the high investment and expenses is weaker profitability, I think we need patience to see if the investment pays off in the long term.

Financials And Earnings Highlights

If there is one area about this company that pleases me, is its revenue performance. Well, the company closed its FY2025 in June this year with a revenue of $4.7 million, marking a YoY growth of 407.6%.

Its trailing revenue stands at $5.6 million, which is a YoY growth rate of 307.2%. More interestingly, the company has maintained at least a triple-digit revenue growth for the last five consecutive quarters, which signals its strong revenue momentum. In my view, this solid topline growth is a testament to a strong demand for its solutions, which I believe justifies the company’s expansion plan. With persistent efforts by the company, such as getting into a consortium to access the market, I expect revenues to keep rising in the coming years. However, I don’t expect the company to sustain its triple-digit revenue growth in the long term due to market saturation. I believe over time it may fall to a strong double-digit growth, which I think is also attractive.

Most importantly, this being a loss-making company, it is important to evaluate the company’s cash runway. With a cash of about $38 million and a trailing cash from operations of about -$1 million, it shows that this company has strong liquidity to sustain its current cash burn, and this shows that the company is on strong financial footing to execute its investment plan.

Moving to earnings, the weight of developing and scaling its manufacturing is felt here. You note that the company’s EPS for Q2 2026 is estimated to hit negative $0.05, and this is expected to bottom further to negative $0.06 until Q1 2027.

Now, the picture of a 6-12 month timeline can be seen closely because this EPS decline continues to June 2027, which is within that range. In June 2027, EPS is estimated to bottom at negative $0.25, representing -32.43%, revealing that the company is likely to experience higher expenses, which its rising revenue can’t offset, leading to lower profitability. This is the basis of my hold rating because until its investment shows a material impact on the bottom line, I expect volatility in the stock.

Investment Take

The company’s momentum reflects this operational spending, which has continued to drive the company’s earnings deeper. Also, I believe the company's continued public offering, for example, with the latest, which raised $25.4 million in gross proceeds, has impacted the stock direction. So far, the company has diluted its outstanding shares from 13 million to 14 million to fund its transition to commercialization, which is becoming a capital-intensive process.

These aspects have deteriorated the company’s stock performance from a 9-month price return of 94.31% to a 3-month price return of -34.53%, which is currently below the sector’s average (-2.81%), a significant difference.

Even further, the relative strength shows that the Aeluma price is in the bearish zone (below 50) and trending downwards. At 36.7, the RSI is yet to hit the oversold region of 20 and as such, with the current EPS weakness and rising expenses, I expect the current downtrend to continue in the mid and short terms. This further forms the basis for my hold recommendation.

Investment Risks

Execution Risk

If you look at the R&D, it also spiked from $0.15 million in Q4 2025 to $0.61 million in Q1 2026, representing a 306.7% QoQ increase. This shows that the company’s capital towards manufacturing preparedness is rising. As such, any missteps in the execution of the planned expansion could see weaker market sentiments sustaining a long term bearish momentum. The company is transitioning from a lab-scale operation to a commercial output in a period of around 6-12 months, which demands additional talent, validation protocols, and new equipment.

As such, I think that delays or unforeseen challenges are likely to prolong this period, and investors may continue experiencing deepening earnings. The wafer-scale processing and backend packaging are complex processes that may need time to achieve the required qualification. Even though it has ample cash, a delay in commercialization may threaten its cash runway, as this is a capital-intensive venture.

Industry Qualification Bottleneck Risk

The go-to-market strategy of Aeluma hinges on the qualification of its semiconductor for quantum computing, aerospace, and defense. Since these sectors impose rigorous standards, this could result in long lead times. Failure to meet these qualifications risks prolonged commercialization outside the projected window. This may continue stalling revenue generation and weaken its promising high-growth momentum.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my hold stance because, despite the company showing high-growth potential, it is currently at an intensive capital injection point towards commercialization. The focus is right now on shifting from lab-scale to commercial operations, which is why I think it warrants a hold rating.

data.title: Waste Management: A Defensive Core Holding With Underappreciated Growth Power
data.description:
Although Waste Management, Inc. (WM) looks like a defensive, boring, and slow-moving stock to be invested in when growth markets are going strong, it has its merits even in a growth portfolio. The defensive credentials are true and may walk into any portfolio on its recession-resistant, even AI-resistant appeal (portfolio stabilizer). However, the Buy thesis does not stop at that. Despite rallies in the past couple of years, valuations haven't exploded (more of a rally in line with fundamentals). So, the fear of heights and questions around whether it is too late to invest in WM look like a minor concern.

I see enough room for continued growth, even if the ESG optionalities do not lead to explosive growth in the future. And just that level of continued incremental growth and improvements has seen WM perform very well (beyond the stable expectations) many times in the past. In fact, I see a lot of scope for capital appreciation (in addition to a stable dividend) over a long-term horizon of 2-3 years, particularly starting from where we are now.

What We Know About Waste Management

WM's business model is centered around collection, transfer, recycling, and disposal services—inherently defensive. Waste generation is essential and recurring, giving the company predictable revenue even during economic slowdowns. Long-term contracts, regional scale, and a regulatory framework that limits competition and further strengthens its defensive appeal.

This stability is very visible in its financials. The TTM revenue graph hardly shows any material contraction through 2008 and 2020. In fact, revenue growth has accelerated in the past 5 years. Even the TTM revenues today show a ~12% increase against the full year 2024 numbers. WM’s revenue growth has been driven by long-term supportive factors like population growth and urbanization. However, in the past 5 years, the re-acceleration in topline is additionally due to several evolving factors: aggressive pricing, Advanced Disposal (AD) acquisition (adding customers and routes inorganically), recycled commodity price increases (cardboard, plastics, and metal rose sharply in 2021-22), and new sustainability initiatives.

WM's utility like FCF and EBITDA margin stability and overall growing trajectory also supports its strong defensive thesis. EBITDA margins in particular are showing stable increasing trends in the past 5 years. FCF has been a little more volatile due to sustainability investments but still remains predictable and growing overall.

Valuations Are Still Reasonable

WM's EV to forward EBITDA is now at ~14.4x, at average levels compared to the 3-year data visible in the charts below. Even the 5-year average is almost exactly what the ratio looks like today. So, historically, valuations are not looking expensive. All the ~80% gains in WM share prices are therefore attributed to real topline and earnings improvements rather than a rerating story.

In fact, EBITDA margins have only moved higher post-COVID and remain at reasonably strong levels despite recent marginal contractions. The concerning metric is a decline in FCF yields despite rising revenue and a healthy EBITDA—FCF yield has compressed meaningfully over the past decade—from ~4% (pre- and immediately post-COVID) to roughly 2.1% today. However, this decline is not the result of operational weakness. WM has invested in fleet modernization, renewable natural gas projects, and recycling upgrades. This is supportive of its pricing power (as seen in topline growth) and long-term efficiencies. Effects of any AD acquisition or working capital swings are not structural.

According to management commentary in the Q3 2025 earnings call, the easing of capex should lift FCF, which has already been improving in the past couple of quarters.

Notably, our operating EBITDA to free cash flow conversion approached 42% in the third quarter, reflecting that we have moved from peak investment levels in sustainability growth projects, landfill infrastructure, and our fleet into a period where we will harvest strong returns on these investments.

Looking ahead, along with FCF improvements, core price improvements of 6% look well above inflation. WM emphasized that price realization and contract repricing remain strong across commercial and industrial customer segments. Q3 growth in revenues was in the mid-teens, and even industrial demand picked up after years of stagnation. Management also emphasized margin improvements to offset any demand softness (guidance has upgraded EBITDA margins).

I think that what you're left with is recognizing that with softness on the revenue line, there had to be outperformance in our execution, particularly in the collection and disposal business on margin, and that's what lifts our confidence in the top end of that range.

Outlook Bullish—Use Long-Term Lens

WM’s revenue guidance for FY2025 was moved to the low end of the prior range, driven entirely by softer recycled commodity prices and modest weakness in WM Healthcare Solutions. Importantly, this adjustment does not reflect deterioration in WM’s core business. Pricing remains strong, volumes are broadly stable, and the collection–landfill engine continues to deliver margin expansion. The revenue softness is centered in low-margin categories, so the EBITDA and FCF impact is limited. In fact, WM raised its free cash flow outlook as the company moves past peak investment levels, confirming that underlying cash generation is improving even as headline revenue fluctuates. Guidance projects FCF for the full year to be ~$2.85b at the midpoint, continuing improvements that could push the FCF yield towards ~2.5%.

Overall, as markets have retraced WM share prices on this weaker guidance, I feel the opportunity for fresh entries becomes even stronger now. As RNG projects contribute further cash and capex normalizes, the FCF rebound looks good. Revenue softness is not across the board and there is additional support coming in from sustainable (slow ramp, but multi-year revenue visibility).

Since I view WM from a long-term lens, any short-term pressure due to recycling commodity prices or retraction in industrial volumes does not make the long-term Buy thesis any weaker. I still see high-double digit growth in 2026 even if demand remains soft—levels that have supported similar valuations in the past for WM (2023 revenue growth was in fact in lower single digits with similar valuations at play). This expectation of high-single digit growth is in line with the implied ~8.5% revenue growth guided for Q4 2025 (derived from full-year guidance minus realized revenues in the past 3 quarters).

Lessons From History

WM's defensive case is well known and appreciated. What is less appreciated is its growth-like total return potential. This is not just a future story but has been seen multiple times in the past. In the 3-year price returns chart below, we can see the average 3-year return mark has often been in the 40-60% range. Even 80% returns are not a rarity—this propels its positioning almost into a growth trajectory.

Interestingly, the minimum 3-year return has never been below ~20%. Add a stable yield of ~1.5% (growing for the past 21 years, last 5-year growth of ~8.5% itself beating inflation comfortably), and investors have rarely reaped anything less than 7-8% over a 3-year horizon. The combination of defensiveness, share price increasing on fundamentals over narratives (no valuation rerating burden), and possibility of 20-25% CAGR returns over 3 years makes a compelling case for WM.

The critical aspect of this outlook is that the 3-year return data has not seen unusual valuation bubble-led returns or required explosive growth or margin expansions. For WM, steady has done it more often than not. Now we have a revenue softness that is nowhere near historical lows. On the other hand, we have a better thesis on margins and cash flow. Interestingly, the current 3-year return is only ~29%, towards the lower end of WM's historical returns. Investments now are late—they are timing it quite close to the best entry point for a long-term horizon.

WM becomes a Strong Buy because of the macro conditions as well, when economic uncertainties and AI rallies require a strong defense in the portfolio. WM is that stabilizer that assures decent returns with the possibility of significant upside, too. For investors wary of short-term dynamics, I recommend systematic accumulation over the next couple of quarters. I am looking from a long-term perspective and do see a good opportunity now overall. I have purposefully taken longer time frame charts to drive home the story of where WM is relative to history and how history has waded through worse with great results. I do not see any concerns around short-term drags (if any) and timing fresh entries.
